content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 10, 1991

Allan E. Tebbetts
Carlsmith Ball Wichman Murray
Case Mukai & Ichiki
301 East Ocean Blvd., Suite 700
Long Beach, CA 90802-4828

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-91-186

Dear Mr. Tebbetts:

You have requested confirmation of oral advice provided to
you on March 18, 1991, relating to the status of certain
regulations promulgated under the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1 In view of the general nature of the advice requested,
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2

This will confirm the advice provided with respect to the
current status of Regulations 18623 and 18728. Specifically, our
interim advice is that these regulations continue in effect
notwithstanding recently-enacted legislation dealing with gifts
and honoraria. (Sections 89501-89507.)3 However, these
regulations will be presented in the Commission for prenotice
discussion of possible amendments at the May Commission meeting.
Other regulations interpreting the new gift and honoraria limits
are also expected to be presented for prenotice discussion at that

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

3 The first sentence of each regulation purports to define the
term "honoraria." However, these definitions of "honoraria"
should be considered modified by the definition of "honorarium"
set forth in Section 89502.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-91-186
Page 2

time.  Therefore, it is possible that this advice may change in
the near future.

     Whether Regulations 18623 and 18728 will be applicable to
participants of the Seventeenth Annual Institute of Governmental
Advocates Summer Conference scheduled for August 8-10, 1991, will
depend upon whether the Commission acts to modify these
regulations or directs staff to modify its advice as to their
continued viability prior to that time.

     In our conversation of March 18, 1991, you indicated that
participants who make a speech or participate in a panel or
seminar may be provided with food and accommodations, but that the
Institute of Governmental Advocates (IGA) would not be paying
their travel expenses.  A Regulations 18623 and 18728 currently
state that necessary accommodations provided directly in
connection with an event at which the official speaks or
participates in a seminar are not reportable as gifts or income.

     We have previously advised that the term "accommodations"
includes lodging and meals. (Puglia Advice Letter, No. A-77-464;
Chel Advice Letter, No. A-78-264, copies enclosed.) The extent to
which food and lodging are "necessary" in connection with speaking
at an event must be considered on a case-by-case basis. For
example, if an official speaks at an event in the morning, lodging
might be required for the night before the speech, but lodging
might not be necessary for the night of the speech. (See, Foreman
Advice Letter, No. I-89-323, copy enclosed.)

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter, you
may contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin

                                        ____________________
                                        By: Margaret W. Ellison
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MWE:dg

Enclosures


_____________________
4  With respect to travel expenses, please note that Regulations
18623 and 18728 provide a limited exception dealing only with
intrastate travel.  Since the conference is scheduled to take
place in Nevada, the exception for travel payments contained in
these regulations would not be applicable.
